CAPE ISELIN — A research vessel returned to port on Wednesday carrying the first clear photographs of a deep-sea coral garden discovered last month on the slope of an underwater canyon two thousand metres beneath the surface, a find scientists are calling the richest cold-water reef ever documented in the region.

The images, captured by a remotely operated submersible tethered to the research ship Meridian, show dense thickets of pale pink and orange coral rising in terraces along the canyon wall, threaded with brittle stars and slow-moving crabs. In one photograph, a bright red fish hovers beside a coral fan nearly two metres across, its branches fine as lace.

"We expected a few scattered colonies," said Dr. Ana Petrova, the marine biologist who led the expedition. "What we found instead was a living wall stretching more than a kilometre along the canyon, in places so thick you could not see the rock beneath it. It is the kind of thing you spend a career hoping to witness once."

Cold-water corals grow in permanent darkness, feeding on particles drifting down from the sunlit waters far above. They build their reefs at a glacial pace — some of the colonies photographed on the canyon wall may be several thousand years old, the researchers estimated, judging by their size and the known growth rates of similar species.

The submersible spent nine days mapping the reef, its lights sweeping across the coral as it moved slowly along the canyon at a depth where the water hovers just above freezing and the pressure would crush an unprotected human body in an instant. The team collected small samples for study and left the rest untouched.

The discovery has already prompted calls to protect the canyon from bottom trawling, a fishing method that drags heavy nets across the seafloor and can flatten a coral reef in a single pass. Conservation groups petitioned the regional fisheries council on Wednesday to declare the canyon off-limits to trawlers while the reef is studied.

Dr. Petrova said her team would return next year with better cameras and a longer tether to survey the deeper reaches of the canyon, which the submersible could not fully explore. "We saw perhaps a third of it," she said. "The rest is still down there in the dark, waiting."

For now, the researchers are cataloguing thousands of photographs and hours of video. Among the images released Wednesday was one that has already spread widely: a single translucent shrimp perched on a coral branch, caught in the submersible's lights against the black water beyond, looking for all the world like a jewel set on a bough of stone.
